text: Hi, welcome to This is My Architecture.
My name is Andrea and I'm here with Heath from Muller Water Products.
Hi, Heath.
Hello.
Welcome to the show.
Thank you.
What do you guys do?
Muller Water Products is a company that's been around for about 150 years.
We focus on water distribution, infrastructure, and intelligence.
Wonderful.
Today we're going to talk about your water intelligent platform.
That's right.
What is that?
Our water intelligent platform ingests data from a large scale IoT network of meters,
smart fire hydrants, and pressure monitoring and water quality devices.
Wonderful.
So let's dive straight to it, shall we?
Sure.
I see a fire hydrant.
What do you collect there?
At our fire hydrants, we're collecting acoustic data that measures noises that are occurring
on the pipe network.
I see.
Okay, so let's walk through a use case.
Sure.
We're collecting information.
What happens next?
Daily, these devices record acoustic signals on the pipe network.
They're correlated so that we have correlated signals between multiple fire hydrants.
Those files are pushed through API gateway on a daily basis.
Okay.
And then what do you do with that?
So you're pushing into API gateway.
Does that persist then in S3?
It does, yes.
Okay.
We have authentication and security at the API gateway layer.
I see.
And those files are then pushed and persisted in S3.
I see.
So why could you not just take that data and just push it straight to S3?
Primarily because of the security around API gateway.
I see.
We focus very heavily around IoT security.
Okay.
So you're getting the data in S3 and what happens next?
The next process that kicks off is a batch process that occurs on a daily basis where each of the files is processed as a node pair to look for correlations in the acoustic signals.
Okay.
And that happens in ECS.
That's right.
That's the container service.
Did you have microservices before?
What was the reason rationale behind using microservices?
Why not just the coded function like Lambda?
Right.
These are very processor intensive acoustic algorithms that occur.
So we felt like a microservice was the best approach.
Okay.
That makes sense.
So process intensive and then you do then some preprocessing.
What happens then?
I see SageMaker.
Mm-hmm.
Walk us through that example.
Sure.
So our ECS services are designed to find correlations and also find correlations that occur over many days that we call persistent correlations.
These persistent correlations are then further classified using a SageMaker ML endpoint that will assign a probability to that persistent correlation.
Okay.
And that probability gives you, say, a leak, potential leak probability?
That's right.
Okay.
So what do you do with that information?
So the main purpose of this entire workflow is to enable our human analysts to focus on the noise sources that are of most importance to us.
I see.
So from the SageMaker endpoint, we get a leak score, a probability, and then we feed that to our human analyst team who will further classify that leak.
Okay.
Now, the analyst, I see an analyst here, they're receiving that score.
What do they do with that information?
So they'll look at the persistent correlation.
They'll talk with the water utility and make sure that there's not some other nearby noise source that could be producing that.
And then they'll, once they've qualified that, then they'll update the status of that leak to a field investigation is required.
Okay.
I see.
Is there any opportunity here for you to look at enhancing this process in terms of, you know, if it's a false positive?
Do you have any mechanisms for dealing with situations where there has been an alert, but, you know, it turns out that there hasn't been a true alert?
Sure.
And that does happen from time to time.
We actually use feedback from the field about every six weeks to further classify and retrain the model so the models get better and better over time.
We're also looking at bringing in additional data points into the process so that we can measure things like flow and pressure in conjunction with acoustic
leak detection data.
Oh, wonderful.
So what is the true benefit here for your end consumer of this platform?
The benefit is protection of non-revenue water loss and also there's an ecological benefit because we certainly don't want chlorinated water spilling out into fresh waterways, etc.
Oh, wonderful.
So what does the future hold for this platform?
Sure.
The future is, I think, a really heavy focus on ML and analytics, bringing in additional data sources and providing real distribution, water distribution intelligence for our customers.

Thank you so much for walking us through this architecture, your intelligent water platform where you're collecting information, sensory information, processing it, and making predictions for water leak.
Thank you for being on the show and thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
